evals.prompt: Run headless. Use the bmad-security-decision-framing skill. Frame the open security-architecture decision described in the system brief at evals/files/secdec/system-brief.md. Run all four rounds without pausing, produce the complete framing report per the skill's output template (boundary/lens map, option table with recommendation, decision entries, follow-on flags), mark all decision entries DRAFT since no human has chosen, and append the Gate questions section listing what each gate would have asked. Use only facts stated in the brief.
evals.expected_output: A complete headless framing report: a boundary/lens map showing the Concierge flow as a Lethal Trifecta hit and a 3/3 Rule-of-Two violation with each mark attributed to a named tool/input; an option table containing at least a planner/quarantined-split option and a capability-stripping or approval-gating option, each with residual risk and cost weighed against the two-instance and low-friction constraints; one recommendation with rationale; DRAFT decision entries shaped as ID/Invariant/Supersedes/Surface terms; a Gate-questions section for GATE 1 and GATE 2; and follow-on flags naming an injection-boundary review and bmad-decision-propagation.
expectations: The boundary/lens map identifies all three Lethal Trifecta legs AND attributes each to the component/tool that earns it from the brief: private-data access earned by mail-read (mailbox contents) and/or pay-invoice (payments account); untrusted-content exposure earned by incoming email bodies and/or web-fetch page content; external communicate/act earned by mail-send and/or pay-invoice. A run that marks a leg without naming its earner fails this expectation.

The Rule-of-Two violation is explicitly called out: the current single-agent architecture holds all three properties ([A] untrustworthy inputs, [B] sensitive access, [C] state change/external action) in one flow with no human in the loop, so it exceeds the at-most-two rule and a human gate (or restructure) is mandatory. Merely listing properties without stating the violation fails.

The option table contains at least one split/quarantine option (e.g. planner + capability-stripped reader of untrusted content) AND at least one capability-stripping or human-approval-gating option, and EVERY option row states a residual risk — what an attacker can still do under that option. An option with no residual-risk statement, or a table missing either option family, fails.

At least one option's cost/complexity assessment engages with a constraint actually stated in the brief (the two-model-instance budget or the low-friction requirement for routine replies) rather than generic cost language.

Each decision entry is marked DRAFT (human has not chosen) and contains all of: an ID, an 'Invariant (now true)' single statement, a 'Supersedes' line naming the old assumption (e.g. the single agent holding all four tools), and 'Surface terms' listing concrete strings (tool names such as mail-send, pay-invoice, agent/capability labels). A SETTLED-status entry, or an entry missing any of the four fields, fails.
The output includes a Gate questions section listing, separately for GATE 1 and GATE 2, the questions/confirmations that would have been put to the human in facilitated mode.

The report does not invent system facts absent from the brief: it must not assert a specific email provider, an existing human-approval step (the brief says there is none today), payment limits other than $2,000 per invoice, or named components/tools beyond mail-read, web-fetch, mail-send, pay-invoice and the single Concierge agent. Architectural proposals are options, not asserted current facts; any unverifiable system fact stated as current truth fails this expectation.

The follow-on flags name bmad-decision-propagation (with its applicability stated, e.g. 'no prose docs exist yet' or the docs that would need it) and an injection-boundary review as the verification step once the flow is designed.
